**1 INTRODUCTION****ABSTRACT** Severalstrategies have been proposed for test quality measurementA softwaretesting consultancyrecentlyreportedon an analysis and analysis. Code coverage is likely the most widely used one. Itof 606 software failures from 314 companies, aimed to understand enables to verify the ability of a test case to cover as many sourcethe impact of test failures on commercial and financial fields11 ]. [ code branches as possible. Although code coverage has been widelyThey found that software failures affected over 3.6 billion people, used, novel strategies have been recently employed. It is the caseresulting inafinanciallossofUS$1.7trillionforcompaniesin of test smells analysis, which has been introduced as an affordablevarious sectors (finance, retail, consumer technology, services and strategy to evaluate the quality of test code. Test smells are poortransportation)in the year of 2017. This example illustrates the need design choices in implementation, and their occurrence in test codefor employing software testing techniques indevelopment might reduce the quality of test suites. Test smells identificationprocesses, as they could anticipatethe detection,correction,and is clearly dependent on tool support, otherwise it could become athe likely effects, ofbugs still during implementation (or even when cost-ineffective strategy. However, as far as we know, there is noexisting functionalities are under evolution) [4, 8, 10]. tool that combinescode coverageand test smells to address testIn a well-defined software engineering process, test code is ex- quality measurement. In this work, we present theTest,apected to co-evolve together with production code, as high-quality tool aimed to analyze test suite quality in the perspectiveof testtest codeisessentialtoeasethemaintenanceandevolutionof smells.JNose Testdetects code coverage and software evolutionboth, production and test code5[14 ]. However, it might be both metrics and a set of test smells throughout software versions.time-consuming and cost-ineffective [5, 14]. Severalapproaches have been proposed in the literature to assess **CCS CONCEPTS**the quality of test suites. For example, code coverage measurement has beenwidelyused to checkthe qualityof automatedtests. It•**Software and**itsengineering→testingandde- measures the test suite quality based on how much it covers struc-**bugging ; Empirical software validation**. tural elements, such as functions, instructions, branches, and lines of code []. Nonetheless, even with a high code coverage, the test**KEYWORDS** code might encompasspoor design choices in their implementa-Quality of Tests, Test Suite Evolution, Test Smells, Code Coverage tion, the so-called test smells. The presence of smells in test code **ACM**may reduce the quality of test suites and, consequently, the Tássio Virgínio, Luana Martins, Larissa Rocha, Railana Santana, Adriana of the production code2[ Additionally, poorly-writtentests can Cruz, be difficultto comprehend,which make it onerous for testers toIn 34th Brazilian Symposiumon Software Engineering(SBES ’20), October maintain the code and detect faults [1, 4].21–23, 2020, Natal, Brazil.ACM, New York, NY, USA, 6 pages. [https://doi](https://doi). The softwaretestingliteraturehasintroducedasetoftoolsorg/10.1145/3422392.3422499 focused onvalidatingthequalityoftestsuites,mainlythrough 1metrics analysis. For example,CodeCoveris an open source Java tool for code coverage executed via both graphical user interface 2(with EclipseIDE)andcommand-line;tsDetectis acommand- line tool for test smells detection.Other tools use code coverage results to predict test smells, such as[ 7 ] and TeCReVis [ 6 ]. Generally, these tools have many different data outputs, which Permissionmight be hard for testers to establish a relationship between code classroom coverageand internal test code quality. Moreover, there are severalfor ontypes of test smells that have not been investigated in conjunction must with codecoverageyet, butcouldalsoprovideopportunitiestoto improve test code quality.fee. In this paper, we introducetheJNose Test , a tool to analyze(iii) Data Output, presents the quality of test suites, in the perspectiveof test smells.JNose.csvfile with the results of the analysis. Testprovidesan automated test strategy focused on (i) identifying possible test design flaws, (ii) analyzing the quality evolution of a**2.1 Processes** softwareproject, and (iii) reducing the effort for performing quality The JNose Test assurance of a test suite. opment Kit (JDK) 1.8 and Maven 3 (or superior). providesthe progress view (Figure 4 - Step 2). Finally, it generates a .csvfile with the results of the data analysis by test smells (Figure 4 - Step 3). Unlike the previous analysis, we provide the exact method where the test smell is located. Each row of.csvtherepresents a test smell, and it has six columnsto show the type of parameter collected, the project name, the test class and the production projectlocation, the test smell name, the method of the testlocation typesin number of line, and the method name (Table 2). of test (3) Evolution.To analyze the project by versions, the**Project** **Analysis process executes**the Git Miningmodule. The tool is implemented as a Java project and comprisesIn thedtopackage, we have the classes used for the transfer of four packages: (i)core,responsible for the detection of test smellsdata amongthe layers of the projects.This packageimplements and coverage metrics; (ii)**page , responsible for presenting the web****Data Input, Project Analysis, Data Output**(Figure 1). 9pages and their content; (iii)**dto , encompassing the classes used in**In thisproject,theApacheMavenwas usedtomanagethe data transfer (Data Transfer Object); and (iv)**util , responsible for**dependencies,build and run theJNose Testtool. Furthermore, the identifyingthe tests and production classes, and saving results intoJNose Testexecutionuses parallel processes, i.e., for each uploaded the .csvfiles.project, the tool creates a new thread, for each test class the tool The corepackageimplements the**Project Analysis, which was**createsanother thread, and so on. With the parallel processing, the split into three other packages, as follows:JNose Testtool can be used to analyze a massive set of projects in a short time period [12].•**Coverage. It applies the business rules necessary**to calcu- late codecoverage.Basically,itidentifiesthetestclasses 6**3 EXAMPLE**OF USErelated to a production class and runs the JaCoCo library. JaCoCo is an open-source library to calculate code coverageIn a previous work, we carried out an experimental study to verify in Java language. JaCoCo performs a dynamic analysis of thethe correlationbetweenthe coverage metrics and test smells. To production code branches (BC), instructions (IC), lines (LC),perform this study, we selected eleven software projects, in which complexity(CC), and methods (MC) to determine which onewe collected twenty-one test smells and five coverage metrics using is either missed or covered by the test code [12];the JNose Test[12, 13]. •**TestSmellDetector**. Thus, thefollowingstepsweretaken:(i)selectalltypesoftest48}**catch**( finalIOExceptionList 49verifysmells; (ii) select the project path; and (iii) enable code coverage. 50assertEqualsThe tool returned 58 test classes. To understand the test smell type 51assertEquals diffusion, we checked the number of classes where each test smell**class**). 52}was present. For example, theException Catching Throwing (ECT) 53} test smell was present in 23 classes, followedAssertionbyRoulette (AR) in 17 test classes, andEager Test (ET) in 16 test classes. Each**Listing 1: ProxyCollectionWriterTest test class** type of test smell can occur many times in a test class. These three types of test smells presented the highest occurrence in the project, counting 316, 175, and 157 times, respectively. Table 4showsfivetestclasseswhichhavethehighestnum- ber of ECT, AR and ET types of test smells. For example, the test class ProxyCollectionWriterTest contains the highest number of those three test smells. Additionally, most test classes achieved good code coverage, when considering the IC, LC and MC coverage metrics (>70%). Therefore, even with a high coverage, the test code might present a low-quality.
